topics.title: Dynamic Programming
topics.story: Richard Bellman invented dynamic programming in the 1950s — he chose the name to make it sound impressive to government funders, hiding that it was just 'math.' The core idea: if you've solved a problem before, don't solve it again — just look up the answer. DP is recursion + memory. The art is identifying what the 'problem' is (the state) and how smaller problems build up to the final answer.
topics.analogy: DP is like the 'I've seen this exact puzzle configuration before' moment in a chess game — you immediately know the best move from memory, instead of thinking through the whole tree again. The first time costs O(n) moves to think; every subsequent identical position costs O(1) to look up.
topics.memoryAnchor: Two signals that DP applies: (1) Overlapping subproblems — the recursion tree has repeated subtrees. (2) Optimal substructure — the optimal solution to the whole problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Both must be true.
topics.firstPrinciples: Top-down (memoization): write naive recursion, add a cache. Bottom-up (tabulation): fill a DP table iteratively from base cases. State = the unique parameters that identify a subproblem. Transition = how dp[i] is computed from smaller states. Base case = simplest subproblem (dp[0] or similar).
topics.bruteForce: Fibonacci: 2^n recursive calls. Coin change: try all subsets = exponential. LCS: try all subsequences = 2^(m+n). Any problem whose brute force is exponential and has the two DP signals is a DP candidate.
topics.optimizedApproach: State identification is the key skill. For 1D problems: dp[i] = answer for first i elements. For 2D: dp[i][j] = answer using first i items from A and first j from B. Space optimization: rolling array when dp[i] only depends on dp[i-1].
topics.whenToUse:
• Count number of ways to reach a target
• Find minimum/maximum cost/length
• Check if something is possible
• Partition/split problems
• Substring/subsequence problems on two strings
• Pattern: 'how many ways', 'minimum steps', 'longest subsequence'

examples.title: Fibonacci — Top-Down Memoization (DP Intuition)
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"if (n <= 1) return n;","variables":{"n":5,"memo":"{}"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"fib(5): n>1, not cached. Will compute fib(4)+fib(3)."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"long result = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2);","variables":{"n":5,"subproblems":"fib(4), fib(3)"},"phase":"RECURSE","annotation":"Recurse: fib(4)→fib(3)→fib(2)→fib(1)=1,fib(0)=0→fib(2)=1→fib(3)=2→fib(4)=3."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"if (memo.containsKey(n)) return memo.get(n);","variables":{"n":3,"memo":"{2:1}","cached":"fib(3) already computed"},"phase":"CACHE_HIT","annotation":"When computing fib(5)=fib(4)+fib(3): fib(3) was already cached during fib(4) computation. Return 2 instantly."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"memo.put(n, result);","variables":{"memo":"{2:1,3:2,4:3,5:5}"},"phase":"MEMOIZE","annotation":"Cache fib(5)=5. Without memo: 2^n calls. With memo: n unique subproblems, each solved once."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"// Bottom-up: no recursion","variables":{"prev2":3,"prev1":5,"result":5},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Bottom-up avoids stack overflow for large n. Both approaches: O(n) time. Top-down O(n) stack space, bottom-up O(1) space."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["fib(5)"] --> B["fib(4)"]
    A --> C["fib(3) — CACHED after left branch"]
    B --> D["fib(3)"]
    B --> E["fib(2)"]
    D --> F["fib(2)"]
    D --> G["fib(1)=1"]
    F --> H["fib(1)=1"]
    F --> I["fib(0)=0"]
    E --> J["fib(1)=1"]
    E --> K["fib(0)=0"]
    style C fill:#fff3cd
    style F fill:#fff3cd
examples.pseudocode:
// Top-Down Memoization:
FUNCTION fib(n, memo={}):
  IF n <= 1: RETURN n         // base case
  IF n in memo: RETURN memo[n] // cache hit
  result = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
  memo[n] = result            // store result
  RETURN result

// Bottom-Up Tabulation:
FUNCTION fibBottomUp(n):
  dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1
  FOR i=2 to n: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
  RETURN dp[n]
examples.explanation: Memoization = 'cache what you've computed'. Without it: fib(5) recomputes fib(3) twice, fib(2) three times — exponential redundancy. With memo: each of the n unique subproblems is solved exactly once → O(n). Two signals that DP applies: (1) overlapping subproblems (same computation repeated), (2) optimal substructure (answer built from sub-answers). Fibonacci has both.
examples.realWorldUse: Every DP problem is a generalization of this pattern. The transition from naive recursion → memoization → bottom-up tabulation → space optimization is the universal DP journey. Used in: compiler optimization (memoize parsed results), game theory (minimax with memo), bioinformatics (sequence alignment).

examples.title: Coin Change — 1D Bottom-Up DP
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"int[] dp = new int[amount + 1]; Arrays.fill(dp, amount+1);","variables":{"coins":"[1,5,6]","amount":11,"dp":"[0,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞,∞]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"dp[0]=0 (zero coins for amount 0). dp[1..11]=∞ (sentinel meaning 'not yet computed')."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp[a] = Math.min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1);","variables":{"a":1,"coin":1,"dp[1]":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"a=1: try coin=1 → dp[1]=min(∞, dp[0]+1)=1. dp=[0,1,∞,...]."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp[a] = Math.min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1);","variables":{"a":5,"dp":"[0,1,2,3,4,1,...]"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"a=5: try coin=1 → dp[4]+1=5. Try coin=5 → dp[0]+1=1. Min=1. dp[5]=1."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp[a] = Math.min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1);","variables":{"a":6,"dp[6]":1},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"a=6: coin=6 → dp[0]+1=1. dp[6]=1 (single coin 6!)."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp[a] = Math.min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1);","variables":{"a":11,"dp[11]":2},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"a=11: coin=5 → dp[6]+1=2. coin=6 → dp[5]+1=2. Answer: 2 coins (5+6=11)."},{"line":14,"lineCode":"return dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount];","variables":{"dp[11]":2},"phase":"COMPLETE","answer":2,"annotation":"dp[11]=2 ≤ amount=11, so return 2. O(amount * |coins|) time, O(amount) space."}]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION coinChange(coins, amount):
  dp = array[amount+1] filled with amount+1  // 'impossible'
  dp[0] = 0

  FOR a = 1 to amount:
    FOR each coin in coins:
      IF coin <= a:
        dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin] + 1)

  RETURN dp[amount] if dp[amount] <= amount else -1

// Recurrence: dp[a] = 1 + min(dp[a-c]) for all coins c
// 'Use coin c, then solve a-c optimally'
examples.explanation: dp[a] = minimum coins to make amount a. Recurrence: try each coin c ≤ a, take 1 coin plus the optimal solution for a-c. Forward loop allows reusing coins (unbounded knapsack). Sentinel amount+1 represents 'impossible' and is distinguishable from valid answers (valid ≤ amount). O(amount × |coins|) time.
examples.realWorldUse: Cash register change making. Network packet fragmentation (minimize number of packets). Breaking a task into minimum number of fixed time slots. The 0/1 knapsack variant (each coin used at most once) requires backward loop instead. This exact pattern appears in: word break, perfect squares, combination sum IV.

examples.title: 0/1 Knapsack — 2D DP
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"int[][] dp = new int[n+1][capacity+1];","variables":{"weights":"[2,3,4,5]","values":"[3,4,5,6]","capacity":5,"n":4},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"4 items, capacity=5. dp[i][w]=max value using first i items within weight w."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w];","variables":{"i":1,"w":1,"dp[1][1]":0},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=1 (item weight=2, value=3): w=1, can't fit (2>1). dp[1][1]=dp[0][1]=0."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"dp[i][w] = Math.max(dp[i][w], values[i-1] + dp[i-1][w - weights[i-1]]);","variables":{"i":1,"w":2,"dp[1][2]":3},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=1,w=2: fit! max(dp[0][2]=0, 3+dp[0][0]=3+0=3) = 3. dp[1][2]=3."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"dp[i][w] = Math.max(...)","variables":{"i":2,"w":5,"dp[2][5]":7},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=2 (weight=3,value=4),w=5: skip→dp[1][5]=3. Take→4+dp[1][2]=4+3=7. Max=7."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"return dp[n][capacity];","variables":{"dp[4][5]":7},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"dp[4][5]=7 (items 1+2: weights 2+3=5, values 3+4=7). Optimal selection found. O(n×W) time and space."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["State: dp[i][w]"] --> B{"Can we take item i?\nweights[i-1] <= w"}
    B -- No --> C["Skip: dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]"]
    B -- Yes --> D["Max of:\nSkip: dp[i-1][w]\nTake: values[i-1]+dp[i-1][w-weight]"]
    C --> E["Fill table row by row"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Answer: dp[n][capacity]"]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION knapsack(weights, values, capacity):
  n = len(weights)
  dp = 2D array[n+1][capacity+1], all zeros

  FOR i = 1 to n:
    FOR w = 0 to capacity:
      dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]       // option 1: skip item i
      IF weights[i-1] <= w:        // can we fit item i?
        take = values[i-1] + dp[i-1][w - weights[i-1]]
        dp[i][w] = max(dp[i][w], take)  // option 2: take it

  RETURN dp[n][capacity]
// Space optimization: 1D dp array with backward loop
examples.explanation: 0/1 knapsack: each item can be taken at most once (hence backward loop in space-optimized version). State dp[i][w] = best value achievable using the first i items with weight capacity w. Two choices at each item: skip (inherit from dp[i-1][w]) or take (value[i] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i]]). The 'i-1' in the take formula ensures we don't reuse the same item. O(n×W) time and space.
examples.realWorldUse: Resource allocation: which projects to fund within budget. Portfolio selection: which stocks to buy within capital limit. Feature selection in ML: maximize model quality within compute budget. Cutting stock problem. The same DP table structure appears in: subset sum, partition equal subset, target sum.

examples.title: Longest Common Subsequence — 2D DP
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"int[][] dp = new int[m+1][n+1];","variables":{"text1":"ABCDE","text2":"ACE","m":5,"n":3},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"LCS of 'ABCDE' and 'ACE'. dp[i][j] = LCS length of first i chars of text1 and first j chars of text2."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"if (text1.charAt(i-1) == text2.charAt(j-1))","variables":{"i":1,"j":1,"char1":"A","char2":"A","dp[1][1]":1},"phase":"MATCH","annotation":"i=1,j=1: 'A'=='A' match! dp[1][1]=1+dp[0][0]=1. LCS so far: 'A'."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]);","variables":{"i":2,"j":1,"char1":"B","char2":"A","dp[2][1]":1},"phase":"SKIP","annotation":"i=2,j=1: 'B'!='A'. max(dp[1][1]=1, dp[2][0]=0)=1. Best LCS without B-A pair."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"text1.charAt(2)=='C'==text2.charAt(1)=='C'","variables":{"i":3,"j":2,"dp[3][2]":2},"phase":"MATCH","annotation":"i=3,j=2: 'C'=='C' match! dp[3][2]=1+dp[2][1]=2. LCS: 'AC'."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"text1.charAt(4)=='E'==text2.charAt(2)=='E'","variables":{"i":5,"j":3,"dp[5][3]":3},"phase":"MATCH","annotation":"i=5,j=3: 'E'=='E' match! dp[5][3]=1+dp[4][2]=3. LCS: 'ACE', length 3."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"return dp[m][n];","variables":{"result":3},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"LCS length=3. O(m×n) time and space. To reconstruct: trace back through dp table."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["State dp[i][j]: LCS of text1[0..i-1] and text2[0..j-1]"] --> B{"text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]?"}
    B -- Match --> C["dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]"]
    B -- No Match --> D["dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])"]
    C --> E["Fill table O(m×n)"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Answer: dp[m][n]"]
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION LCS(text1, text2):
  m=len(text1), n=len(text2)
  dp = 2D array[m+1][n+1], all zeros

  FOR i=1 to m:
    FOR j=1 to n:
      IF text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]:    // chars match
        dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]  // extend LCS
      ELSE:                            // skip one char
        dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])

  RETURN dp[m][n]
// Reconstruct: trace back — if match, go diagonal; else go in max direction
examples.explanation: LCS is the canonical 2D DP. State dp[i][j] answers 'what is the LCS of the first i characters of text1 and first j characters of text2?' Two cases: if last chars match, extend the LCS of remaining prefixes by 1. If not, try skipping from either string and take the best. The table fills bottom-up, each cell depending on 3 neighbors. O(mn) time and space.
examples.realWorldUse: Git diff (Myers algorithm, LCS-based). DNA sequence alignment (edit distance variant). Plagiarism detection. File comparison (diff utility). Spell checker (edit distance = LCS-based). Code refactoring tools: minimum changes between two versions. The same 2D DP structure appears in: edit distance, shortest common supersequence, longest palindromic subsequence.

examples.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence — 1D DP + Binary Search
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"int[] dp = new int[n]; Arrays.fill(dp, 1);","variables":{"nums":"[10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]","dp":"[1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1]"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"dp[i]=LIS ending at nums[i]. Every element alone is a LIS of length 1."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"if (nums[j] < nums[i]) dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1);","variables":{"i":3,"nums[3]":5,"j":2,"nums[2]":2,"dp[3]":2},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=3 (val=5): j=2 (val=2<5) → dp[3]=max(1, dp[2]+1)=2. LIS ending at 5 is [2,5]."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"if (nums[j] < nums[i]) dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1);","variables":{"i":5,"nums[5]":7,"dp[5]":3},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"i=5 (val=7): best predecessor is 5 (dp[3]=2) → dp[5]=3. LIS: [2,5,7]."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"dp result","variables":{"dp":"[1,1,1,2,2,3,4,4]","maxLen":4},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"dp=[1,1,1,2,2,3,4,4]. Max=4. One LIS: [2,3,7,101] or [2,5,7,101]."},{"line":20,"lineCode":"int pos = Collections.binarySearch(tails, num);","variables":{"tails":"[2,3,7,18]","num":101,"result":4},"phase":"OPTIMAL","annotation":"O(n log n) patience sort: tails grows to length 4. tails.size()=LIS length. Tails not the actual LIS, but length is correct."}]
examples.pseudocode:
// O(n²) DP:
FUNCTION lisDP(nums):
  dp = array[n] all 1s
  FOR i=1 to n-1:
    FOR j=0 to i-1:
      IF nums[j] < nums[i]:
        dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1)
  RETURN max(dp)

// O(n log n) Patience Sort:
FUNCTION lisOptimal(nums):
  tails = []
  FOR num in nums:
    pos = binarySearch(tails, num)  // first position >= num
    tails[pos] = num  // replace or append
  RETURN len(tails)
examples.explanation: O(n²) DP: dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. For each i, scan all j < i — if nums[j] < nums[i], we can extend the LIS ending at j by appending nums[i]. O(n log n) patience sort: maintain 'tails' array where tails[i] = smallest tail of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. Binary search to find where to insert/replace. The length of tails equals the LIS length.
examples.realWorldUse: Stock trading (longest non-decreasing price streak). Version control: minimum number of insertions to make a sequence sorted. Box stacking problem (3D variant). Scheduling jobs by deadline. The patience sorting insight is also used in: finding minimum number of piles to sort a deck of cards.

problems.title: Climbing Stairs
problems.description:
You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can climb 1 or 2 steps. How many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

**Example:**
n=2 → 2 (1+1, 2)
n=3 → 3 (1+1+1, 1+2, 2+1)
problems.hint1: To reach step n, you came from step n-1 (one step) or step n-2 (two steps). How many ways can you reach each of those?
problems.hint2: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). This is Fibonacci! Base cases: ways(1)=1, ways(2)=2.
problems.hint3: Bottom-up: just track the last two values. O(1) space.
problems.editorial: Fibonacci recurrence: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). Base cases: n=1→1, n=2→2. Space-optimized O(1) using two variables. This is the simplest DP — the key insight is recognizing the recurrence relation.

problems.title: House Robber
problems.description:
You are a robber. Houses are in a line; you cannot rob two adjacent houses. Given nums[i] (money in each house), find the maximum amount you can rob.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,3,1] → Output: 4 (rob 1 and 3)
Input: [2,7,9,3,1] → Output: 12 (rob 2,9,1)
problems.hint1: For each house i, you either rob it or skip it. What are the two choices, and what does each give you?
problems.hint2: dp[i] = max money robbing up to house i. Either skip house i (take dp[i-1]) or rob it (take nums[i] + dp[i-2]).
problems.hint3: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). Space-optimized with two variables.
problems.editorial: Standard DP: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]). Optimized to O(1) space using prev1 and prev2. Transition covers both cases: skip current (take prev1) or rob current (add nums[i] to prev2 — skipping the adjacent).

problems.title: Coin Change
problems.description:
Given an integer array coins (denominations) and an integer amount, return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. Return -1 if impossible.

**Example:**
coins=[1,5,11], amount=15 → Output: 3 (5+5+5)
coins=[2], amount=3 → Output: -1
problems.hint1: Think of dp[a] as the minimum coins for amount a. What's dp[0]? How does using one coin transform dp[a-coin] into dp[a]?
problems.hint2: dp[a] = min over all coins of (dp[a-coin] + 1), if a >= coin and dp[a-coin] is reachable.
problems.hint3: Initialize dp with a sentinel (amount+1). dp[0]=0. For each coin, iterate forward (unbounded — can use same coin multiple times). Answer: dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount].
problems.editorial: Unbounded knapsack DP: forward loop allows using same coin multiple times. Sentinel amount+1 represents 'impossible' — always larger than any valid answer (max coins = amount ones). O(amount * coins.length) time, O(amount) space.

problems.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

**Example:**
Input: [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18] → Output: 4 ([2,3,7,101])
Input: [0,1,0,3,2,3] → Output: 4
problems.hint1: dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i. What's the base case? How do you build dp[i] from previous dp values?
problems.hint2: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]. Start with dp[i]=1 (LIS of just nums[i]).
problems.hint3: Answer = max of all dp[i]. O(n²) approach. For O(n log n): maintain a 'tails' array and binary search for insertion position.
problems.editorial: O(n²) DP: dp[i] = LIS ending at i. O(n log n) patience sort: maintain tails[] where tails[i] = smallest tail element of all IS of length i+1. Binary search for insertion point. The length of tails = LIS length.

problems.title: Edit Distance
problems.description:
Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert word1 to word2.

**Example:**
word1='horse', word2='ros' → Output: 3
(horse→rorse: replace h with r, rorse→rose: delete r, rose→ros: delete e)
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = minimum operations to convert word1[0..i] to word2[0..j]. What are the base cases?
problems.hint2: If word1[i-1] == word2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no operation needed). Otherwise: min of replace (dp[i-1][j-1]+1), insert (dp[i][j-1]+1), delete (dp[i-1][j]+1).
problems.hint3: Base cases: dp[i][0] = i (delete i characters), dp[0][j] = j (insert j characters). Space-optimize to O(min(m,n)) using a rolling array.
problems.editorial: Levenshtein distance: 2D DP with three transitions. Space-optimized to O(n) using rolling array — curr[j] depends on prev[j-1], prev[j], curr[j-1] which are all available in the 1D arrays. O(m*n) time, O(min(m,n)) space.

problems.title: Word Break
problems.description:
Given a string s and a dictionary wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

**Example:**
s='leetcode', wordDict=['leet','code'] → true
s='applepenapple', wordDict=['apple','pen'] → true
s='catsandog', wordDict=['cats','dog','sand','and','cat'] → false
problems.hint1: dp[i] = can the first i characters of s be segmented? What does dp[0] equal?
problems.hint2: dp[i] is true if there exists j < i where dp[j] is true AND s[j..i] is in the dictionary.
problems.hint3: dp[0] = true (empty string). For each i from 1 to n, check all j from 0 to i: if dp[j] && wordDict contains s.substring(j, i), set dp[i] = true.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 300 | 1 ≤ wordDict.length ≤ 1000 | 1 ≤ wordDict[i].length ≤ 20 | All strings contain only lowercase letters | No duplicates in wordDict
problems.editorial: DP on string prefixes. dp[i] = can s[0..i] be segmented. Transition: find a split point j where dp[j] is true and s[j..i] is in the dictionary. Using HashSet for O(1) lookup. O(n² * L) where L = average word length (substring creation). Trie optimization reduces this.

problems.title: Unique Paths
problems.description:
A robot is on an m×n grid at the top-left corner. It can only move right or down. Count the number of unique paths to the bottom-right corner.

**Example:**
m=3, n=7 → Output: 28
m=3, n=2 → Output: 3
problems.hint1: How many ways can you reach cell (i, j)? What cells can you come from?
problems.hint2: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] (came from above or left). Base cases: first row and first column all = 1.
problems.hint3: Space-optimize to O(n) using a single row array: dp[j] += dp[j-1] (in-place update, left-to-right).
problems.editorial: Classic 2D DP reduced to 1D: dp[j] = paths to reach current row's column j. Adding dp[j-1] (from left) to existing dp[j] (from above — left over from previous row iteration). O(m*n) time, O(n) space. Mathematical formula: C(m+n-2, m-1) also works.

problems.title: Longest Common Subsequence
problems.description:
Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence (LCS). A subsequence doesn't need to be contiguous.

**Example:**
text1='abcde', text2='ace' → Output: 3 (ace)
text1='abc', text2='abc' → Output: 3
text1='abc', text2='def' → Output: 0
problems.hint1: dp[i][j] = LCS of text1[0..i] and text2[0..j]. What happens when text1[i] == text2[j]?
problems.hint2: If text1[i-1] == text2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 (extend LCS by one). Otherwise: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) (skip one character from either string).
problems.hint3: Base cases: dp[0][j] = dp[i][0] = 0 (empty string has 0 LCS with anything). Fill row by row.
problems.editorial: 2D DP space-optimized to O(n) with two rolling rows. When characters match: extend from diagonal (prev[j-1]). When they don't match: take best from either skip (max of above or left). Foundation for git diff, DNA alignment, spell correction.

problems.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums, return true if you can partition it into two subsets with equal sum.

**Example:**
Input: [1,5,11,5] → Output: true ([1,5,5] and [11])
Input: [1,2,3,5] → Output: false
problems.hint1: If total sum is odd, impossible. Otherwise, find if any subset sums to total/2 — this is a subset sum problem.
problems.hint2: dp[t] = can we form sum t using elements so far? Start dp[0]=true. For each num, iterate t from target down to num: dp[t] |= dp[t-num].
problems.hint3: Backward iteration (like 0/1 knapsack) ensures each element is used at most once.
problems.editorial: Subset sum DP: target = sum/2. Backward loop (0/1 knapsack pattern) ensures each element used once. O(n * target) time, O(target) space. Early termination possible: if dp[target] becomes true, return immediately.

problems.title: Decode Ways
problems.description:
A message encoded as digits can be decoded: 'A'=1, 'B'=2, ..., 'Z'=26. Given a string of digits, return the total number of ways to decode it.

**Example:**
Input: '12' → Output: 2 ('AB' or 'L')
Input: '226' → Output: 3 ('BZ', 'VF', 'BBF' = 'BBF'... 'B'+'B'+'F'=226, 'B'+'Z'=226, 'V'+'F'=226)
Input: '06' → Output: 0
problems.hint1: dp[i] = number of ways to decode first i characters. What are the transitions?
problems.hint2: One-digit decode: if s[i-1] != '0', dp[i] += dp[i-1]. Two-digit decode: if s[i-2..i-1] forms a valid code (10-26), dp[i] += dp[i-2].
problems.hint3: Base cases: dp[0]=1 (empty string = 1 way). dp[1]=1 if s[0]!='0' else 0. Be careful with leading zeros: '01' is invalid.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 100 | s contains only digits | s may contain leading zeros
problems.editorial: Two-state DP (climbing stairs variant): dp[i] depends only on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], so use two variables. Key edge cases: '0' alone is invalid (no single-digit decode), two-digit must be 10-26. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Burst Balloons
problems.description:
Given an array of balloons nums, each with a number. Burst all balloons. When you burst balloon i, you gain nums[i-1] * nums[i] * nums[i+1] coins (use 1 if out of bounds). Return the maximum coins obtainable.

**Example:**
Input: [3,1,5,8] → Output: 167
(Burst 1: 3*1*5=15, Burst 5: 3*5*8=120, Burst 3: 3*8*1=24, Burst 8: 1*8*1=8 → 15+120+24+8=167... optimal order gives 167)
problems.hint1: Thinking about which balloon to burst FIRST leads to complex state (because neighbors change). Try thinking about which balloon to burst LAST in a range.
problems.hint2: dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons between indices i and j (exclusive). Try every k as the LAST balloon to burst in [i,j].
problems.hint3: When k is the last burst in [i,j]: coins = nums[i]*nums[k]*nums[j] (because i and j are still standing as boundaries). Add dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]. Pad nums with 1s at boundaries.
problems.editorial: Interval DP: dp[i][j] = max coins in open interval (i,j). 'Last balloon k' reversal avoids the state explosion of 'first burst' thinking. Padding with boundary 1s handles the boundary cases cleanly. O(n³) time, O(n²) space.
